summary: Fournier argues that adding product managers to infrastructure organizations won't magically create a product culture—the entire engineering culture must change. Infrastructure teams excel at cost management and technical depth but typically treat internal users as a captive audience rather than customers. The transformation requires updating support models, interview processes, recognition systems, and how engineers spend their time. She warns against over-relying on project managers and emphasizes that engineers must own migrations and customer relationships directly. The shift parallels the DevOps transformation: it asks more of engineers but delivers higher-quality outcomes.
key_points: Product managers alone cannot fix a culture problem—engineering teams must feel ownership for delivering great products to customers
Infrastructure teams must change how they provide support, with engineers regularly answering questions to understand customer pain
Recognition and reward systems must value usability work and customer empathy, not just hard technical problems
Heavy reliance on project managers masks poor migration UX and prevents engineers from building automation that would help customers
Engineers will spend more time talking to customers and less time purely writing code, with an up-front productivity cost that pays off over time
money_quotes: You can't just rub product managers on it and call it a day.

If the engineering teams don't feel a sense of ownership for delivering a great product to their customers, product managers are unlikely to close that gap, and they will more likely turn into glorified backlog groomers than true product leaders.
The ticket system black hole is a great way to make your customers feel more like a burden than a focus.
If you have someone you can't trust to engage productively in help situations, watch out, because this person is probably not building products that are easy to use.
If you only promote people who solve big technical problems, you're going to have a hard time retaining the people who do the work to smooth out the usability edges.
Yes, migrations are part of your UX!
It is simply not much fun to have an antagonistic relationship with your users, and to feel like you are deep inside of an us-vs-them dynamic with colleagues.
Engineers in a product-focused organization do not build software without consideration of the users of that software.
key_insight: Transforming infrastructure organizations into product-focused teams requires changing engineering culture itself—how you hire, reward, support customers, and structure work—not just adding product managers to existing processes.
